questions.text: Why does evaluating a model's performance on the same validation sets used to select optimal hyperparameters (e.g. via grid search) result in selection bias?
options: The model weights are directly updated via gradient descent on the validation samples.
Choosing the maximum score across multiple hyperparameter configurations fits the hyperparameters to the validation sets, yielding an optimistically biased evaluation.
Validation sets are drawn from a different probability distribution than training sets, skewing the parameters.
Grid search algorithms restrict hyperparameters to integer values, underestimating model capacity.
questions.correct_answer: Choosing the maximum score across multiple hyperparameter configurations fits the hyperparameters to the validation sets, yielding an optimistically biased evaluation.
questions.explanation: Tuning parameters involves selecting the configuration $\theta^*$ that maximizes performance on the validation folds. Because the validation score is the maximum of a set of random variables, it is statistically biased upward. The validation set is no longer independent of the model selection process, meaning reported performance will be overoptimistic.

questions.text: Under what condition is Stratified Group K-Fold cross-validation preferred over standard Stratified K-Fold?
options: When the target variable is continuous and has high variance.
When the dataset contains multiple correlated samples belonging to the same entity (group) and we want to prevent group-level data leakage.
When the training run requires parallel processing across multiple GPUs.
When there is no class imbalance and we want to speed up training splits.
questions.correct_answer: When the dataset contains multiple correlated samples belonging to the same entity (group) and we want to prevent group-level data leakage.
questions.explanation: If a dataset contains multiple records from the same group (e.g. multiple physiological measurements from the same patient), standard cross-validation will distribute these correlated samples across both training and validation folds. The model can memorize group characteristics, inflating validation scores while failing on new groups in production. Group K-Fold guarantees that all samples from a group are placed in the same fold.

questions.text: In time-series modeling, why is random K-Fold cross-validation prohibited, and how do walk-forward splits prevent leakage?
options: Random splits are too computationally expensive compared to sequential splits.
Random K-Fold splits break the temporal ordering, allowing training on future values to predict the past, and leak target values through autocorrelation.
Walk-forward splits dynamically re-scale features inside the validation loop to force stationarity.
Random splits prevent the loss function from computing gradients because sequence lengths must remain constant.
questions.correct_answer: Random K-Fold splits break the temporal ordering, allowing training on future values to predict the past, and leak target values through autocorrelation.
questions.explanation: Time-series data exhibits temporal dependency. Randomly splitting data allows target lookahead leakage: a training sample at time $t+1$ is used to predict a validation sample at time $t$. Autocorrelation also allows the model to interpolate validation targets from adjacent training observations. Walk-forward validation enforces that all training data precedes validation data in time, preserving temporal causality.
